title: Contagious vs Made to Stick: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Compare Contagious vs Made to Stick. Jonah Berger's viral marketing vs Chip & Dan Heath's sticky ideas - which framework helps your message spread?

book1.title: Contagious
book1.subtitle: Why Things Catch On
book1.author: Jonah Berger
book1.description: Jonah Berger, a Wharton marketing professor, reveals why certain products and ideas go viral while others flop. He presents the STEPPS framework: Social Currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical Value, and Stories. Berger shows that virality isn't random or about luck—it's driven by six principles that can be deliberately applied. This research-based book explains how to engineer word-of-mouth and create contagious content that people naturally share.

book2.title: Made to Stick
book2.subtitle: Why Some Ideas Survive and Others Die
book2.author: Chip Heath and Dan Heath
book2.description: The Heath brothers explore why some ideas stick in people's minds while others are forgotten. They present the SUCCESs framework: Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories. Using examples from urban legends to successful marketing campaigns, they show how to craft messages that are memorable and impactful. This book focuses on making ideas sticky—ensuring they're remembered and acted upon long after the initial exposure.

table.book1: Get people to share and talk
table.book2: Get people to remember and act
Urban legends, memorable campaigns, sticky teaching

book1Strengths: STEPPS memorable: Social Currency (make people look good), Triggers (top of mind = tip of tongue), Emotion, Public, Practical Value, Stories
cheesesteak example perfect: Philly restaurant created  sandwich, went viral not because good but gave customers social currency (status)
Rebecca Black Friday analysis: even 'bad' content goes viral with strong emotion. Song got 167M views because made people feel something
Practical Value explains why 'How to save money' articles shared more than opinions. Usefulness inherently shareable across networks
Backed by Berger's Wharton research analyzing thousands of NYT articles finding what makes content most shared. Data-driven, not anecdotal
12,000 ratings at 4.5 stars shows proven popular and accessible. More relevant for 2020s—covers social media, viral videos, digital word-of-mouth
book1Weaknesses: Viral doesn't equal valuable. Rebecca Black's Friday got 167M views, career went nowhere. STEPPS explains spread, not quality or lasting value
Social Currency can backfire. Trying too hard to be cool makes brands look desperate.  cheesesteak works for shock, most can't sustain gimmicks
Focuses on SHARING not REMEMBERING. If people share but forget in 2 days, did it work? Made to Stick ensures memorability, not just clicks
Triggers principle HARD to engineer. Can't control what reminds people of brand daily. 'Top of mind = tip of tongue' sounds great but how?
Examples skew consumer products:  cheesesteak, Blendtec blenders, viral videos. B2B or services have fewer applicable cases than Made to Stick
book2Strengths: SUCCESs framework itself sticky: Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories. The acronym makes you remember all 6 elements
Kidney heist urban legend analysis brilliant: why believe waking in bathtub missing kidney? Hits all 6 SUCCESs elements. Unforgettable example
Curse of Knowledge concept: once you know something, can't imagine not knowing it. Worth book price for teachers and leaders alone
Velcro Theory of Memory: abstract concepts don't stick, concrete examples do. Brain has more 'hooks' for concrete ideas than abstractions
Works beyond marketing—teachers, executives, nonprofits use SUCCESs for presentations, training, change management. Universal application
Timeless principles working since 2007 and still today. Human psychology of memory hasn't changed. Will work in 2030s too
book2Weaknesses: Published 2007 PRE-social media explosion. Facebook 3 years old, Twitter 1 year, no Instagram. Barely mentions digital. Contagious built for viral age
291 pages versus Contagious's 256. Heath brothers tell 3 stories where Berger tells 1. More repetition and tangents. Some want STEPPS's tighter focus
Less popular (9,500 versus 12,000 ratings) and lower rating (4.4 versus 4.5). Contagious resonates more with modern marketers doing digital
Examples skew OLD: kidney heist, Nordstrom tire return, Southwest Airlines. Pre-2000s examples. Contagious uses YouTube, social—more relevant 2020s
Less emphasis on SHARING mechanisms. Makes ideas memorable but doesn't explain HOW to trigger word-of-mouth. Remember but don't necessarily share

book1: Word of mouth is more effective than advertising because it's more persuasive and more targeted.
Making things more observable makes them easier to imitate, which makes them more likely to become popular.
If something is built to show, it's built to grow.
Top of mind means tip of tongue.
People don't think in terms of information. They think in terms of narratives.
book2: The most basic way to get someone's attention is this: Break a pattern.
If you say three things, you don't say anything.
Abstraction makes it harder to understand an idea and to remember it.
The Curse of Knowledge: when we know something, we find it hard to imagine what it was like not to know it.
Stories are told and retold because they contain wisdom.
For an idea to stick, for it to be useful and lasting, it's got to make the audience care.

book1: You want to create viral content or campaigns
You need to understand modern word-of-mouth marketing
You're focused on social media and digital sharing
You want research-backed principles for virality
You need content that people will naturally share
You're in marketing or growth roles
You want to engineer word-of-mouth deliberately
book2: You want ideas that people remember long-term
You're a teacher, presenter, or communicator
You need to make internal messages stick with teams
You're interested in the psychology of memory
You need to change behavior, not just get shares
You want stories that illustrate principles beautifully

verdict.summary: Contagious wins for modern marketers—12,000 ratings at 4.5 versus 9,500 at 4.4. STEPPS (Social Currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical Value, Stories) built for viral social media age. Published 2013, covers YouTube, digital sharing. Made to Stick wins for communicators and teachers—SUCCESs (Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories) makes ideas MEMORABLE for presentations, teaching, behavior change. Read Contagious for virality, Made to Stick for memorability.
verdict.recommendation: Read Contagious first if building viral campaigns, managing social media, or creating shareable content. STEPPS framework gives checklist: Social Currency ( cheesesteak makes people look cool), Triggers (top of mind = tip of tongue), Emotion (Rebecca Black Friday got 167M views from strong feelings), Public (built to show = built to grow), Practical Value (useful info spreads), Stories (people share narratives). At 256 pages, backed by Berger's Wharton research analyzing thousands of NYT articles. After Contagious, read Made to Stick if you're teacher, presenter, or need ideas remembered long-term. SUCCESs framework (Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories), Curse of Knowledge (can't imagine not knowing once you know), kidney heist urban legend (hits all 6 elements), Velcro Theory of Memory (concrete sticks, abstract doesn't) work universally. At 291 pages published 2007, it's pre-social media but timeless for memory psychology. If you only read one: Contagious for viral marketing. Made to Stick for memorable communication and teaching.
